modules.name: Programming Concepts
modules.description: Python fundamentals — variables, control flow, functions, file operations, and automation basics.

projects.title: University Folder Organizer
projects.description: Automatically create your university course folder structure for the semester.
explanation: Line 1-2: Import os and Path from pathlib. Path is the modern way to handle file paths — it's object-oriented and works across all operating systems.
Line 5: Path.home() returns your user directory. We append 'MUST' as the root university folder. Works on Windows, Linux, and Mac.
Lines 8-13: Dictionary mapping course codes to their required subfolders. This is your configuration layer — edit to match your actual courses each semester.

Lines 15-19: Loop through each course, create Year1 folder, then create all subfolders. mkdir(parents=True) creates parent directories automatically. exist_ok=True prevents errors if folders already exist.
Line 18: The / operator on Path objects joins paths cleanly. Equivalent to os.path.join() but more readable.

modules.name: Computer Systems Architecture
modules.description: CPU architecture, memory hierarchy, storage systems. Build system profilers and hardware monitors.

projects.title: System Hardware Profiler
projects.description: Display detailed CPU, RAM, and disk information — your own Task Manager replacement.
projects.code:
import psutil
import platform

def system_profiler():
    print('=' * 50)
    print('  SYSTEM HARDWARE PROFILER')
    print('=' * 50)
    
    print(f'\n🖥️  CPU:')
    print(f'   Processor: {platform.processor()}')
    print(f'   Physical cores: {psutil.cpu_count(logical=False)}')
    print(f'   Total logical cores: {psutil.cpu_count(logical=True)}')
    print(f'   Max frequency: {psutil.cpu_freq().max:.0f} MHz')
    print(f'   Current usage: {psutil.cpu_percent(interval=1)}%')
    
    mem = psutil.virtual_memory()
    print(f'\n💾 RAM:')
    print(f'   Total: {mem.total / (1024**3):.1f} GB')
    print(f'   Available: {mem.available / (1024**3):.1f} GB')
    print(f'   Used: {mem.percent}%')
    
    print(f'\n💿 Disk:')
    for part in psutil.disk_partitions():
        try:
            usage = psutil.disk_usage(part.mountpoint)
            print(f'   {part.device} — {usage.total/(1024**3):.1f} GB total')
        except:
            pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    system_profiler()
explanation: Line 1: psutil (Python System Utilities) is the standard library for system information. Install with pip install psutil.
Line 11: cpu_count(logical=False) returns physical cores. With hyperthreading, logical cores are typically double the physical count.
Line 13: cpu_freq().max gives maximum clock speed. Some virtual machines don't expose this information.
Lines 17-20: virtual_memory() returns a named tuple. /(1024**3) converts bytes to gigabytes for human-readable output.
Lines 23-28: disk_partitions() lists all drives. disk_usage() shows space. Some partitions need admin rights.

modules.name: Computer Network Engineering
modules.description: OSI model, TCP/IP stack, IP addressing, subnetting. Build network discovery and diagnostic tools.

projects.title: Network Device Scanner
projects.description: Discover all devices on your local network using ARP requests.
projects.code:
from scapy.all import ARP, Ether, srp

def scan_network(ip_range='192.168.1.0/24'):
    arp = ARP(pdst=ip_range)
    ether = Ether(dst='ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff')
    packet = ether / arp
    result = srp(packet, timeout=3, verbose=0)[0]
    
    devices = []
    for sent, received in result:
        devices.append({'ip': received.psrc, 'mac': received.hwsrc})
    return devices

if __name__ == '__main__':
    devices = scan_network()
    print(f'Found {len(devices)} devices:')
    for d in devices:
        print(f'  {d["ip"]:15} — {d["mac"]}')
explanation: Line 1: Scapy is the Swiss Army knife of packet manipulation. ARP handles address resolution, Ether handles frames, srp sends/receives.
Line 4: pdst (protocol destination) sets the IP range to scan. '192.168.1.0/24' scans 254 addresses.
Line 5: 'ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff' is the broadcast MAC — the packet goes to ALL devices on the local network.
Line 6: The / operator stacks layers. This creates an Ethernet frame containing an ARP request.
Line 7: srp() sends and receives at Layer 2. timeout=3 waits 3 seconds. [0] gets only answered packets.

modules.name: Operating Systems
modules.description: Process management, memory management, file systems. Build sysadmin automation and monitoring tools.

projects.title: Process Monitor & Manager
projects.description: List running processes, identify resource hogs, and safely terminate problematic programs.
explanation: Line 3: process_iter() loops through all running processes. Specifying fields improves performance by only retrieving needed data.
Line 6: memory_info().rss is Resident Set Size — the actual physical RAM the process occupies.
Line 10: Lambda sorts by memory usage descending. Change to 'cpu_percent' to sort by CPU instead.
Line 11: [:limit] returns only the top N processes. Prevents overwhelming output on systems with hundreds of processes.

modules.name: Object Oriented Programming
modules.description: Classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism. Model network devices and systems as Python classes.

projects.title: Network Device Class Hierarchy
projects.description: Design an OOP model of network infrastructure — routers, switches, and firewalls as Python classes.
explanation: Lines 1-6: Base NetworkDevice class. __init__ is the constructor — runs when you create a new device. All devices have name, IP, MAC, and online status.
Lines 8-11: ping() method checks if device is reachable. Uses OS ping command — Windows uses -n, Unix uses -c for count.
Lines 13-15: __str__ defines what print(device) displays. Shows colored indicator based on online status.
Lines 17-23: Router INHERITS from NetworkDevice. super().__init__() calls parent constructor. Adds routing table specific to routers.
Lines 25-29: Switch also inherits from NetworkDevice. Has port count and MAC address table.

modules.name: Data Communication
modules.description: Communication protocols, packet structure, data transmission. Analyze network traffic with Python.

projects.title: Packet Sniffer & Analyzer
projects.description: Capture and analyze live network packets — understand TCP/IP by seeing real traffic.
explanation: Line 1: sniff() captures packets. IP, TCP, UDP are layer classes for accessing protocol fields.
Line 4: haslayer(IP) checks if the packet contains an IP layer. Filters out non-IP traffic.
Lines 5-7: Extract source IP, destination IP, and protocol number from the IP header.
Lines 9-12: Check for TCP or UDP layers. Print source/destination IP:port for each packet.
Line 15: prn=analyze_packet sets callback function. count=20 stops after 20 packets. Requires root/admin.

modules.name: Routing and Switching
modules.description: Routing protocols, switching concepts, VLANs. Automate network device configuration with Python.

projects.title: Network Config Backup Tool
projects.description: Automatically backup configurations from multiple network devices via SSH.
projects.code:
from netmiko import ConnectHandler
from datetime import datetime

def backup_device(device_info):
    try:
        conn = ConnectHandler(**device_info)
        hostname = conn.find_prompt().strip('#')
        config = conn.send_command('show running-config')
        
        filename = f'{hostname}_{datetime.now():%Y%m%d}.cfg'
        with open(filename, 'w') as f:
            f.write(config)
        
        conn.disconnect()
        print(f'✅ Backed up: {hostname} -> {filename}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'❌ Failed: {device_info["host"]} — {e}')

devices = [
    {'device_type': 'cisco_ios', 'host': '192.168.1.1', 
     'username': 'admin', 'password': 'cisco'},
]

for device in devices:
    backup_device(device)
explanation: Line 1: Netmiko simplifies SSH connections to network devices. Install with pip install netmiko.
Line 6: **device_info unpacks the dictionary as keyword arguments to ConnectHandler.
Line 7: find_prompt() returns the device prompt. Stripping '#' gives the hostname.
Line 8: send_command() executes a command and returns the output. 'show running-config' dumps the config.
Line 10: Filename includes hostname and date for organization. Format: Router1_20240704.cfg

modules.name: Database Design and Implementation
modules.description: Database design, normalization, SQL. Build database-driven applications with SQLite and Python.

projects.title: Network Inventory Database
projects.description: Complete CRUD system for tracking network devices, locations, and maintenance history.
explanation: Line 1: sqlite3 comes with Python — no installation needed. Perfect for embedded databases.
Lines 4-5: __init__ connects to database file. Creates it if it doesn't exist. Calls create_tables() on startup.
Lines 8-16: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS is idempotent — safe to run multiple times. UNIQUE constraints prevent duplicate entries.
Lines 19-22: Parameterized queries with ? placeholders prevent SQL injection. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
Lines 25-29: LIKE with % wildcards enables partial matching. Search 'sw' finds 'switch', 'SW1', etc.

modules.name: Hardware and Software Integration
modules.description: Hardware-software interfaces, device drivers, firmware. Communicate with hardware using Python.

projects.title: Serial Port Communicator
projects.description: Communicate with hardware devices (Arduino, routers, sensors) via serial port.
explanation: Line 1: pyserial is the standard library for serial communication. Install with pip install pyserial.
Lines 4-8: list_ports.comports() enumerates all serial ports on the system. Returns port name and description.
Lines 11-12: Serial() opens a connection. Baud rate must match the device (9600 is common for Arduino).
Lines 14-16: in_waiting checks if data is available. readline() reads until newline. decode() converts bytes to string.
